nodes.name: Countries of the World
nodes.description: Country sets by region and category

config.prompt: Name all 20 sovereign countries of Central America and the Caribbean

items.explanation:
Twin-island nation in the Lesser Antilles; capital is St. John's on Antigua
Claims to have 365 beaches — one for every day of the year
Renamed its highest peak (402 m) from Boggy Peak to Mount Obama in 2009, in honor of the U.S. president
Home to the world's rarest snake, the critically endangered Antiguan racer, found only on Bird Island

Archipelago of ~700 islands and 2,400 cays in the Atlantic, southeast of Florida; capital is Nassau on New Providence
Only ~30 of the islands are inhabited; the country stretches over 100,000 mi² of ocean but has just ~14,000 km² of land
Nassau was a notorious pirate haven during the Golden Age of Piracy — Blackbeard and Charles Vane operated from its harbor
San Salvador Island is one of the leading candidates for Columbus's first New World landfall in 1492

Easternmost Caribbean island, in the Lesser Antilles; capital is Bridgetown
Parliament established in 1639 — third-oldest continuous parliament in the Commonwealth after Britain's and Bermuda's
Became a republic in November 2021, removing Queen Elizabeth II as head of state; Rihanna was declared a National Hero at the inauguration ceremony
Historic Bridgetown and its Garrison are a UNESCO World Heritage Site

Only English-speaking country in Central America; capital is Belmopan
Formerly British Honduras until independence in 1981
Home to the Belize Barrier Reef, part of the Mesoamerican Barrier Reef System — the second-largest coral reef system in the world after Australia's Great Barrier Reef
The Great Blue Hole, a giant marine sinkhole ~318 m across and 124 m deep, was made famous by Jacques Cousteau as one of the world's top diving sites

Located between Nicaragua and Panama; capital is San José
Constitutionally abolished its military in 1949 after José Figueres broke a wall at the Bellavista Barracks in a symbolic ceremony (December 1, 1948)
Contains ~6% of the world's biodiversity despite covering only 0.03% of Earth's surface
Name means "Rich Coast" in Spanish; one of the most politically stable countries in Latin America

Largest island in the Caribbean (~110,000 km²); capital is Havana
Site of the 1962 Cuban Missile Crisis, the closest the Cold War came to nuclear conflict
Cubans call the island "El Cocodrilo" due to its crocodile shape when viewed from above
Havana, founded in 1519, became the capital in 1552 and served as the staging point for Spanish expeditions to Mexico, Puerto Rico, and beyond
Old Havana (La Habana Vieja) is a UNESCO World Heritage Site with one of the best-preserved colonial-era cityscapes in the Americas

Mountainous volcanic island in the Windward Islands; capital is Roseau — not to be confused with the Dominican Republic
Known as the "Nature Isle of the Caribbean" for its dense rainforest and hot springs
Home to the Boiling Lake, the second-largest thermally active lake in the world (~60–75 m across, water at 82–92 °C)
Morne Trois Pitons National Park is a UNESCO World Heritage Site featuring the Valley of Desolation and Emerald Pool

Occupies the eastern two-thirds of Hispaniola (shared with Haiti); capital is Santo Domingo
Santo Domingo, founded 1496, is the oldest permanent European settlement in the Americas — home to the first cathedral, first university (1538), and first castle in the New World
Served as the launching point for Cortés's conquest of Mexico, Ponce de León's colonization of Puerto Rico, and Balboa's sighting of the Pacific
The Colonial City of Santo Domingo is a UNESCO World Heritage Site

Smallest and most densely populated country in Central America; capital is San Salvador
Only Central American country without a Caribbean coastline — borders only the Pacific
In 2021 became the first country in the world to adopt Bitcoin as legal tender (later scaled back under IMF pressure in 2025)
Known as the "Land of Volcanoes" — has more than 20 volcanoes, several of them active

Southernmost of the Windward Islands; capital is St. George's
Known as the "Island of Spice" — one of the world's largest producers of nutmeg and mace
Invaded by the U.S. in October 1983 (Operation Urgent Fury) after a military coup; the UN General Assembly condemned it 108–9 as a violation of international law
St. George's horseshoe-shaped harbor is considered one of the most picturesque in the Caribbean

Most populous country in Central America; capital is Guatemala City
Heartland of ancient Maya civilization — Tikal, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, was one of the largest Maya cities with pyramids rising to 65 m
The quetzal, the national bird, gives its name to the currency; resplendent quetzal feathers were prized by Maya royalty
Chichicastenango market is one of the largest indigenous markets in the Americas

Occupies the western third of Hispaniola; capital is Port-au-Prince
First free Black republic in the world and second independent nation in the Western Hemisphere (after the U.S.), gaining independence from France on January 1, 1804
The Citadelle Laferrière, built 1805–1820 by formerly enslaved people, is the largest fortress in the Americas and a UNESCO World Heritage Site
The only country where Haitian Creole and French are both official languages

Located between Guatemala, El Salvador, and Nicaragua; capital is Tegucigalpa
Tegucigalpa means "hill of silver" in Nahuatl, named for the silver mines that drew Spanish settlers in the 1570s
One of the few capital cities in the world without a railroad
Home to the Copán ruins, a major Maya archaeological site and UNESCO World Heritage Site renowned for its elaborate stelae and hieroglyphic stairway

Third-largest Caribbean island; capital is Kingston
Birthplace of reggae music and Bob Marley, whose "One Love" (1977) became an anthem for peace worldwide
Blue Mountain coffee, grown at elevations above 900 m, is among the most expensive and sought-after coffees in the world
Usain Bolt, the fastest human ever recorded (9.58 s in the 100 m), hails from Trelawny Parish

Largest country in Central America by area; capital is Managua
Managua was chosen as capital in 1852 to settle the rivalry between León and Granada, two cities that had feuded for centuries
Lake Nicaragua (Cocibolca) is the largest lake in Central America and one of the few freshwater lakes with bull sharks
Devastated by a major earthquake in 1972 that destroyed most of Managua's city center

Connects Central and South America; capital is Panama City
The Panama Canal (completed 1914) allows ships to cross between the Atlantic and Pacific, saving ~12,600 km vs. sailing around South America; ~13,000–14,000 ships transit annually
The Darién Gap is a ~100 km break in the Pan-American Highway — the only place the road from Alaska to Argentina is interrupted
Panama City's skyline is the most prominent in Central America; the country uses the U.S. dollar alongside the balboa as currency

Two-island federation in the Leeward Islands; capital is Basseterre on Saint Kitts
Smallest sovereign state in the Western Hemisphere by both area (~261 km²) and population (~48,000), and the world's smallest sovereign federation
Saint Kitts was the first Caribbean island colonized by both the English (1623) and the French (1625)
Brimstone Hill Fortress National Park is a UNESCO World Heritage Site

Volcanic island in the eastern Caribbean; capital is Castries
The twin volcanic spires called the Pitons (Gros Piton and Petit Piton) are a UNESCO World Heritage Site and the island's most iconic landmark
Has produced two Nobel laureates — economist Sir Arthur Lewis (1979) and poet Derek Walcott (1992) — both born on January 23, giving it more Nobel Prizes per capita than any other country
Changed hands between Britain and France 14 times before finally becoming British in 1814

Multi-island nation in the Windward Islands; capital is Kingstown on Saint Vincent
La Soufrière, the country's highest peak (1,234 m), is an active volcano that erupted explosively in April 2021, displacing ~20,000 people
Wallilabou Bay on Saint Vincent was a primary filming location for Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl (2003)
The Grenadines include the exclusive Mustique island, a retreat for celebrities and British royals

Southernmost Caribbean nation, just ~11 km off the coast of Venezuela; capital is Port of Spain
Birthplace of the steelpan (steel drum), the only acoustic instrument invented in the 20th century, developed from oil drums in the 1930s
Also the birthplace of calypso, soca, and limbo; Trinidad Carnival is one of the largest street festivals in the world
Pitch Lake in southwest Trinidad is the largest natural deposit of asphalt in the world
